questions.questionText: A child presents with intensely itchy papules, burrows, and vesicles on web spaces of fingers, wrists, and genitalia. Multiple family members are affected. Diagnosis?
questions.optionB: Scabies (Sarcoptes scabiei infestation — characteristic burrows in web spaces, intense pruritus worse at night, highly contagious — treatment: permethrin 5% cream)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Scabies = burrows + intense nocturnal itch + family involvement + distribution. Why correct: Sarcoptes scabiei hominis. Classic distribution: web spaces, wrists, elbows, axillae, genitalia (males), around nipples (females). Burrow: sine-wave track with mite at one end. Nocturnal pruritus (mite more active at night). Why A wrong: Contact dermatitis is localized to contact site, no burrows. Why C wrong: Psoriasis — extensor, silvery scales, not contagious. Why D wrong: Eczema — flexural, not contagious, no burrows. Clinical relevance: Diagnosis: mineral oil test (mite/eggs/fecal pellets under microscopy). Treatment: permethrin 5% cream (DOC), ivermectin oral (for crusted/Norwegian scabies or outbreaks). Treat ALL household contacts simultaneously. Wash clothing/bedding at >60°C. Exam trap: Norwegian/crusted scabies = heavily crusted, highly contagious, in immunocompromised — treat with oral ivermectin. Memory aid: 'Scabies = Scratching at night + Sparring in web spaces + Spreading to family.' Reference: IADVL Textbook – Parasitic.
questions.conceptTag: Scabies burrows nocturnal itch permethrin web spaces
